quiz.question: The hospital was sued for negligence after a surgeon operated on the wrong knee. What is negligence?
options: Deliberate misconduct intended to cause harm, as the evidence showed
Failure to take proper care — resulting in harm
Incompetence caused by insufficient training
A breach of contract with a patient
quiz.answer: Failure to take proper care — resulting in harm
quiz.explanation:
Negligence is a failure to take reasonable care, causing harm to another.
• negligence = careless failure to take proper precautions
• From Latin: 'neglegere' — to disregard
• Legal distinction: negligence is careless (not intentional); malpractice is professional negligence

quiz.question: Her diligence in checking every source made the report far more reliable. What is diligence?
options: Exceptional expertise in a specialist subject
Careful, steady effort and attentiveness
A methodical approach to organizing information
Natural aptitude combined with years of experience
quiz.answer: Careful, steady effort and attentiveness
quiz.explanation:
Diligence is careful and persistent effort — steady application and attentiveness.
• diligence = careful and hard-working attention
• From Latin: 'diligere' — to love, value highly
• 'Due diligence' = the careful investigation required before a decision

quiz.question: The restaurant's ambience — soft lighting, jazz, and fragrant flowers — made the evening memorable. What is ambience?
options: The quality of the food and service
The atmosphere and feeling created by a surrounding environment
The decor and physical design of a space
The combination of noise and music in an environment, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The atmosphere and feeling created by a surrounding environment
quiz.explanation:
Ambience is the characteristic atmosphere of a place — its mood and feeling.
• ambience = the overall atmosphere or mood of a place
• From French: 'ambiance' — from Latin 'ambire' (to go around)
• 'Good ambience' = the intangible quality that makes a place feel right

quiz.question: The analyst's prescience about the market crash made her famous. What is prescience?
options: Correct analysis based on historical data
Knowledge of what will happen before it occurs
Detailed expertise in financial markets
Accurate prediction based on sound modeling, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Knowledge of what will happen before it occurs
quiz.explanation:
Prescience is foreknowledge — knowing or seeming to know what will happen before it does.
• prescience = foresight; knowledge of events before they occur
• From Latin: 'prae-' (before) + 'scire' (to know)
• 'Prescient' = showing knowledge of the future; prescience = the quality itself

quiz.question: Her conscience prevented her from staying silent about the fraud she had witnessed. What is conscience?
options: Her professional duty to report irregularities
Her inner sense of right and wrong guiding her behavior
Her fear of being implicated if she said nothing
Her awareness that she could be held legally responsible
quiz.answer: Her inner sense of right and wrong guiding her behavior
quiz.explanation:
Conscience is the inner moral sense that guides behavior — the internal voice of right and wrong.
• conscience = the moral sense of right and wrong; an inner voice
• From Latin: 'conscientia' — shared knowledge, awareness
• 'A guilty conscience', 'in good conscience' — the conscience judges one's own actions

quiz.question: The puppy's ebullience was exhausting — it never stopped jumping and yelping. What is ebullience?
options: An erratic and unpredictable temperament
Irrepressible cheerfulness and lively enthusiasm
A high level of physical energy and stamina, as the evidence showed
A playful refusal to follow instructions
quiz.answer: Irrepressible cheerfulness and lively enthusiasm
quiz.explanation:
Ebullience is irrepressible, bubbling enthusiasm — overflowing liveliness.
• ebullience = exuberant high spirits; enthusiasm that cannot be held back
• From Latin: 'ebullire' — to boil up, bubble out
• 'Ebullient' = bubbly and enthusiastic; ebullience = the quality

quiz.question: The shop was chosen for its convenience — it was directly below her office. What does 'convenience' mean here?
options: Its competitive prices relative to nearby competitors
Its suitability and ease of access for the purpose
Its extended opening hours and wide product range
Its familiarity as a regular part of her routine
quiz.answer: Its suitability and ease of access for the purpose
quiz.explanation:
Convenience is the quality of being easy and accessible — fitting in with one's needs without effort.
• convenience = the quality of being easy to use or access
• From Latin: 'convenire' — to come together, be suitable
• 'At your convenience' = when it suits you; 'convenient' = easy and accessible

quiz.question: The philosopher wrote about the transience of fame — how quickly it is forgotten. What is transience?
options: The shallow nature of celebrity in modern culture
The quality of lasting only a brief time
The difficulty of sustaining achievements over time
The way public attention moves rapidly from one thing to another
quiz.answer: The quality of lasting only a brief time
quiz.explanation:
Transience is the quality of being temporary — passing quickly and not lasting.
• transience = the state of lasting only briefly
• From Latin: 'transire' — to go across, pass
• 'Transient' = passing; 'transience of life' = its brief, impermanent nature

quiz.question: She felt ambivalence about the job offer — excited by the opportunity but reluctant to leave her team. What is ambivalence?
options: A sense of calm acceptance of competing demands
The simultaneous experience of opposing feelings
Uncertainty caused by not having enough information
Reluctance to commit to a decision under pressure
quiz.answer: The simultaneous experience of opposing feelings
quiz.explanation:
Ambivalence is the state of having two conflicting feelings about something at the same time.
• ambivalence = mixed feelings; simultaneous attraction and repulsion
• From Latin: 'ambi-' (both) + 'valere' (to be strong)
• 'Ambivalent' = having conflicting feelings; not simply uncertain

quiz.question: The student's insolence — interrupting the teacher and rolling her eyes — earned her a detention. What is insolence?
options: A disrespectful attitude shown toward one's peers
Rude and disrespectful behavior toward someone in authority
Deliberate disruption of a structured environment
A refusal to follow instructions given by a teacher, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Rude and disrespectful behavior toward someone in authority
quiz.explanation:
Insolence is rude and contemptuous behavior — disrespect toward someone in authority.
• insolence = offensive rudeness; disrespectful impudence
• From Latin: 'insolens' — unusual, arrogant
• 'Insolent' = impertinently disrespectful; insolence = the behavior

quiz.question: The doctor said the patient's flatulence was caused by a change in diet. What is flatulence?
options: Uncomfortable bloating and distension of the abdomen, as the evidence showed
The production and passing of gas in the digestive tract
A burning sensation caused by excess stomach acid
Indigestion that makes eating painful or difficult
quiz.answer: The production and passing of gas in the digestive tract
quiz.explanation:
Flatulence is the accumulation and passing of gas in the digestive system.
• flatulence = gas formed in the stomach and intestines
• From Latin: 'flatus' — a blowing, breaking of wind
• Figuratively: 'flatulent prose' = pompously wordy and full of hot air

quiz.question: The ceremony will commence at noon precisely. What does 'commence' mean?
options: To reach its most important and climactic point
To begin
To be formally opened by a presiding dignitary
To proceed in an orderly and organized fashion
quiz.answer: To begin
quiz.explanation:
To commence means to begin — to start.
• commence = to begin; to start an activity
• From Old French: 'comencier' — to begin
• More formal than 'start' or 'begin'; used in legal, academic, and official contexts

quiz.question: He was a scholar of great eminence whose work shaped his field for decades. What is eminence?
options: A high degree of expertise in a narrow specialism
Fame and superiority in a particular field
A large body of influential published research
The respect that comes from a long academic career
quiz.answer: Fame and superiority in a particular field
quiz.explanation:
Eminence is the state of being distinguished and outstanding — above ordinary level.
• eminence = fame and distinction; a position of superiority
• From Latin: 'eminere' — to stand out, project
• 'Eminent' = outstanding; 'His Eminence' = title for a cardinal

quiz.question: The judge rebuked counsel for the impertinence of questioning the court's authority. What is impertinence?
options: Arguing a point that had already been settled
Rude presumption — going beyond what is appropriate or respectful
Introducing irrelevant evidence in a formal proceeding, as the evidence showed
Failing to follow the proper procedures of the court
quiz.answer: Rude presumption — going beyond what is appropriate or respectful
quiz.explanation:
Impertinence is rudeness — behaving in a way that is not appropriate to one's position.
• impertinence = impudent rudeness; not sticking to what is relevant or respectful
• From Latin: 'impertinens' — not pertaining, not relevant
• An impertinent question = one that oversteps what is appropriate to ask

quiz.question: She spoke with deference to the senior professor, always waiting to be invited to contribute. What is deference?
options: Polite submission and respectful yielding to another's authority
Formal courtesy required in professional settings
A reluctance to speak without being directly asked, as the evidence showed
The respect owed to someone with greater expertise
quiz.answer: Polite submission and respectful yielding to another's authority
quiz.explanation:
Deference is respectful submission to another's authority or judgment.
• deference = courteous submission; yielding to another out of respect
• From Latin: 'deferre' — to carry down; to yield
• 'In deference to' = out of respect for; 'deferential' = showing deference

quiz.question: The engineer calculated the circumference of the pipe before ordering new fittings. What is circumference?
options: The diameter measured across the widest point
The distance all the way around a circular shape
The cross-sectional area of a circular opening
The thickness of the material forming the pipe wall
quiz.answer: The distance all the way around a circular shape
quiz.explanation:
The circumference is the total distance around the outside of a circle.
• circumference = the perimeter of a circle
• From Latin: 'circum-' (around) + 'ferre' (to carry)
• C = 2πr or πd; distinct from radius (center to edge) and diameter (edge to edge through center)

quiz.question: The delegates flew from twelve countries to attend the annual conference. What is a conference?
options: A formal assembly of people to discuss a common topic
A series of lectures given over several consecutive days
A meeting held to resolve a specific dispute or crisis
An international summit attended only by heads of state
quiz.answer: A formal assembly of people to discuss a common topic
quiz.explanation:
A conference is a formal meeting or assembly for discussion or exchange of views.
• conference = a formal meeting of people with a shared interest or purpose
• From Latin: 'conferre' — to bring together
• 'Press conference', 'academic conference' — a gathering for organized discussion

quiz.question: The government was accused of interference in the judiciary's independence. What is interference?
options: Influencing or obstructing something that should be independent
Making appointments to senior positions in a public body
Introducing legislation that affects the operation of courts, as the evidence showed
Publicly criticizing decisions made by independent bodies
quiz.answer: Influencing or obstructing something that should be independent
quiz.explanation:
Interference is unwanted involvement — obstructing or meddling in something.
• interference = the act of obstructing or meddling uninvited
• From Latin: 'inter-' (between) + 'ferire' (to strike)
• 'Political interference', 'radio interference' — both involve unwanted intrusion

quiz.question: The diplomat's belligerence in negotiations made agreement almost impossible. What is belligerence?
options: A refusal to make concessions on key issues
An aggressive and hostile attitude in dealings with others
An unwillingness to follow the established protocol
A tendency to interpret the other side's positions negatively
quiz.answer: An aggressive and hostile attitude in dealings with others
quiz.explanation:
Belligerence is aggressive hostility — a fighting, quarrelsome attitude.
• belligerence = aggressive or warlike behavior; readiness to fight
• From Latin: 'bellum' (war) + 'gerere' (to wage)
• 'Belligerent' = aggressively hostile; belligerence = the quality

quiz.question: She was described as the quintessence of elegance — everything about her was effortlessly refined. What is quintessence?
options: The most visible and celebrated example of a quality
The purest, most perfect embodiment of something
A combination of several admirable qualities in one person
The ideal standard against which others are measured
quiz.answer: The purest, most perfect embodiment of something
quiz.explanation:
The quintessence is the most perfect and pure example of something — its very essence.
• quintessence = the purest form; the ideal embodiment
• From Medieval Latin: 'quinta essentia' — the fifth element (the purest, beyond earth, air, fire, water)
• 'Quintessential' = the most typical and perfect example

quiz.question: The recruiter said the role required competence in three programming languages. What is competence?
options: Expert-level mastery demonstrated through certification
The ability to do something to a required standard
Familiarity with the basics of a subject or skill
Experience using a tool in a professional environment
quiz.answer: The ability to do something to a required standard
quiz.explanation:
Competence is the ability to do something effectively — meeting the required standard.
• competence = having the skill and knowledge needed for a task
• From Latin: 'competere' — to be fit, be adequate
• 'Core competencies', 'professional competence' — the ability to do the job properly

quiz.question: She maintained the pretence of not knowing about the surprise party. What is pretence?
options: A polite fiction maintained to spare someone's feelings
A false appearance deliberately kept up to deceive
An exaggerated reaction performed for someone's benefit
A misunderstanding that was never corrected
quiz.answer: A false appearance deliberately kept up to deceive
quiz.explanation:
Pretence is a false show — a performance maintained to hide the truth.
• pretence = a claim or appearance that is not true; make-believe
• From Old French: 'pretendre' — to hold forward
• 'Under false pretences' = using deception; 'keep up a pretence' = maintain an act

quiz.question: The error was due to inadvertence — the clerk simply failed to check the figure. What is inadvertence?
options: A mistake caused by insufficient expertise or training, as the evidence showed
A failure to notice something through lack of attention
A systematic flaw in the checking procedures used
An error made under pressure and time constraints
quiz.answer: A failure to notice something through lack of attention
quiz.explanation:
Inadvertence is a failure to notice — inattention that leads to an unintentional error.
• inadvertence = failure to notice; an accidental oversight
• From Latin: 'in-' (not) + 'advertere' (to turn toward)
• 'By inadvertence' = accidentally, through inattention rather than intent

quiz.question: The family lived on subsistence farming — growing only enough to eat. What does 'subsistence' mean here?
options: Small-scale farming using traditional methods
The bare minimum needed to maintain life
Self-sufficient farming without buying or selling produce
Farming on poor soil with limited water access
quiz.answer: The bare minimum needed to maintain life
quiz.explanation:
Subsistence means the minimum level needed to survive — just enough to live on.
• subsistence = the minimum requirements for maintaining life
• From Latin: 'subsistere' — to stand still, remain
• 'Subsistence wage' = barely enough to live on; 'subsistence farming' = surviving, not thriving

quiz.question: The stain would not shift despite repeated scrubbing — its persistence was remarkable. What is persistence?
options: Its ability to spread to other areas of the fabric
Its continued existence despite efforts to remove it
Its resistance to the specific chemicals used
Its deep penetration into the fibers of the material, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Its continued existence despite efforts to remove it
quiz.explanation:
Persistence is the quality of continuing — refusing to stop or be removed.
• persistence = continuing firmly despite difficulty or opposition
• From Latin: 'persistere' — to continue steadfastly
• Can be admirable (human persistence) or frustrating (persistence of a problem)

quiz.question: The neighborhood's affluence was obvious from the cars and manicured gardens. What is affluence?
options: A comfortable and well-organized way of life
An abundance of material wealth and prosperity
The social status that comes from a good education
A high quality of life achieved through hard work
quiz.answer: An abundance of material wealth and prosperity
quiz.explanation:
Affluence is wealth — having an abundant flow of money and material resources.
• affluence = great wealth; having more than enough
• From Latin: 'affluere' — to flow toward
• 'Affluent society' = a wealthy one; 'the affluent' = wealthy people

quiz.question: She accepted the consequences of her decision without complaint. What does 'consequences' mean here?
options: The criticism she received from others for her choice
The results and effects that followed from her action
The responsibilities she took on by making that decision
The difficulties she encountered after committing to a path
quiz.answer: The results and effects that followed from her action
quiz.explanation:
A consequence is a result or effect — what follows from a cause.
• consequence = a result or effect produced by a cause
• From Latin: 'consequi' — to follow
• 'Face the consequences' = accept the results; 'of consequence' = of importance

quiz.question: The barrister's eloquence swayed the jury when the evidence alone might not have. What is eloquence?
options: The clarity and accuracy of a legal argument
The fluent, persuasive power of well-chosen words
The confidence to speak clearly in a formal setting
The ability to present complex ideas simply
quiz.answer: The fluent, persuasive power of well-chosen words
quiz.explanation:
Eloquence is fluent, powerful, and persuasive expression — language at its most effective.
• eloquence = the quality of effective, moving, and persuasive speech
• From Latin: 'eloqui' — to speak out
• 'Eloquent' = expressing well; eloquence goes beyond correctness to persuasion and beauty

quiz.question: His calm manner evinced no trace of the anxiety he must have felt. What does 'evince' mean?
options: To deliberately hide a feeling from others
To show clearly the existence of a quality or feeling
To overcome a feeling through self-discipline
To express a feeling only through physical gestures, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To show clearly the existence of a quality or feeling
quiz.explanation:
To evince means to show clearly — to give evidence of a quality.
• evince = to reveal or demonstrate clearly
• From Latin: 'evincere' — to overcome, prove
• 'Evince interest', 'evince concern' — show unmistakably that the quality is present

quiz.question: She ensconced herself in the armchair by the fire and refused to move all evening. What does 'ensconce' mean?
options: To fall asleep quickly in a comfortable position
To settle comfortably and firmly in a place
To claim a desirable spot before others can take it
To wrap oneself warmly before settling down
quiz.answer: To settle comfortably and firmly in a place
quiz.explanation:
To ensconce means to establish comfortably and securely in a place.
• ensconce = to settle into a comfortable, secure position
• From 'sconce' — a shelter or protective structure
• 'Ensconced in the corner' = settled in and hard to dislodge

quiz.question: The human rights group publicly denounced the regime. What does 'denounce' mean?
options: To report a person or regime to the relevant authority
To publicly condemn as wrong or wicked
To refuse to engage with or recognize officially
To formally break off diplomatic relations with
quiz.answer: To publicly condemn as wrong or wicked
quiz.explanation:
To denounce means to publicly declare to be wrong or evil — to condemn openly.
• denounce = to publicly declare wicked or evil; to formally condemn
• From Latin: 'denuntiare' — to announce officially
• Also: to denounce someone = to inform against them officially

quiz.question: The cold wind pierced through her thin jacket. What does 'pierce' mean here?
options: To surround her with cold air on all sides
To penetrate sharply and painfully through something
To make the jacket impossible to keep closed, as the evidence showed
To cut through her jacket with its force
quiz.answer: To penetrate sharply and painfully through something
quiz.explanation:
To pierce means to penetrate sharply — to go through with a pointed force.
• pierce = to go into or through with a sharp instrument or sensation
• From Old French: 'percer' — to bore through
• 'A piercing look', 'piercing cold' — going straight through as if with a sharp point

quiz.question: He was coerced into signing the document by threats to his family. What does 'coerce' mean?
options: To persuade someone against their initial inclination
To compel someone by force or threats — removing free choice
To trick someone into doing something through deception, as the evidence showed
To pressurize someone by exploiting their weaknesses
quiz.answer: To compel someone by force or threats — removing free choice
quiz.explanation:
To coerce means to compel by force or threats — to override someone's free will.
• coerce = to force someone to act against their will using pressure or threats
• From Latin: 'coercere' — to restrain, confine
• 'Coercion' = the use of force or threats; 'coercive' = compelling compliance

quiz.question: The board acquiesced to the shareholders' demands without putting up a fight. What does 'acquiesce' mean?
options: To agree loudly and enthusiastically after careful review
To accept or comply without protesting
To delay a decision until further information is available
To approve formally through a vote at a meeting
quiz.answer: To accept or comply without protesting
quiz.explanation:
To acquiesce means to accept or agree to something without active resistance — quiet compliance.
• acquiesce = to accept without protest; to go along with
• From Latin: 'acquiescere' — to rest in, be satisfied with
• Acquiescence implies passivity — no enthusiasm, but no resistance either

quiz.question: Several small opposition groups coalesced into a single powerful movement. What does 'coalesce' mean?
options: To agree on a shared platform and set of policies
To come together and merge into a unified whole
To form an alliance while maintaining separate identities
To coordinate their activities without formally combining
quiz.answer: To come together and merge into a unified whole
quiz.explanation:
To coalesce means to come together and form one body — to merge.
• coalesce = to combine and form a single, unified mass or group
• From Latin: 'coalescere' — to grow together
• 'Coalescing forces', 'ideas coalesce' — separate elements grow into one

quiz.question: After surgery, she spent two weeks at home to convalesce. What does 'convalesce' mean?
options: To undergo physical rehabilitation following an operation
To recover gradually from illness or surgery
To rest completely and avoid all physical exertion
To receive ongoing medical care from a specialist
quiz.answer: To recover gradually from illness or surgery
quiz.explanation:
To convalesce means to recover slowly — to regain health after illness or surgery.
• convalesce = to recover strength and health gradually
• From Latin: 'convalescere' — to grow strong
• 'Convalescent home' = a facility for recovering patients; convalescence = the process

quiz.question: The tablet effervesced as soon as it was dropped into the water. What does 'effervesce' mean?
options: To dissolve instantly without any visible reaction
To produce bubbles and foam as gas is released
To change color as it reacts with the liquid
To sink slowly to the bottom of the glass
quiz.answer: To produce bubbles and foam as gas is released
quiz.explanation:
To effervesce means to bubble and fizz — to give off gas in small bubbles.
• effervesce = to produce bubbles; to fizz
• From Latin: 'effervescere' — to boil up
• Figuratively: 'effervescent personality' = bubbling with energy and enthusiasm

quiz.question: The pamphlet traduced the candidate with lies about her private life. What does 'traduce' mean?
options: To criticize someone on the basis of their public record
To defame someone by making false and damaging statements
To undermine someone's credibility through repeated attacks
To publish private information about a person without consent
quiz.answer: To defame someone by making false and damaging statements
quiz.explanation:
To traduce means to speak falsely and maliciously about someone — to slander.
• traduce = to speak ill of; to slander or defame
• From Latin: 'traducere' — to lead across, expose to ridicule
• A deliberately cruel and dishonest form of attack

quiz.question: The lawyer adduced several new witnesses to support her client's alibi. What does 'adduce' mean?
options: To pay the fees required to bring a witness to court
To cite or put forward evidence or arguments
To arrange for witnesses to appear at a set time
To challenge the reliability of existing testimony
quiz.answer: To cite or put forward evidence or arguments
quiz.explanation:
To adduce means to cite or offer as evidence — to bring forward proof or argument.
• adduce = to present as an argument, reason, or evidence
• From Latin: 'adducere' — to lead to
• 'Adduce evidence', 'adduce examples' — to put forward in support of a claim

quiz.question: The magician asked a volunteer to produce a coin from his pocket. What does 'produce' mean here?
options: To manufacture a coin using sleight of hand
To bring out and make visible for others to see
To check whether the coin was genuine or fake
To pass the coin to the magician for examination
quiz.answer: To bring out and make visible for others to see
quiz.explanation:
To produce (in this sense) means to bring forward — to present or show.
• produce = to bring something out; to show or present
• From Latin: 'producere' — to bring forward
• 'Produce your passport', 'produce a rabbit from a hat' — to bring into view

quiz.question: The warring sides agreed to a truce over the holiday weekend. What is a truce?
options: A permanent peace agreement following negotiations
A temporary suspension of hostilities by mutual agreement
A ceasefire imposed by an international peacekeeping body
An informal understanding not to attack during a set period
quiz.answer: A temporary suspension of hostilities by mutual agreement
quiz.explanation:
A truce is a temporary agreement to stop fighting — not a permanent peace.
• truce = an agreed suspension of hostilities for a limited period
• From Old English: 'treow' — faith, pledge
• An armistice is formal; a truce can be informal — both are temporary

quiz.question: Her cheerful facade concealed deep anxiety. What does 'facade' mean here?
options: An outward appearance that conceals the true state within
A performance maintained to meet others' expectations, as the evidence showed
A way of behaving that had become habitual over time
A mask worn to avoid difficult conversations
quiz.answer: An outward appearance that conceals the true state within
quiz.explanation:
A facade is an outward appearance that conceals what is really inside.
• facade = a deceptive outward appearance; a false front
• From French: 'façade' — the face of a building
• Literally: the front face of a building; figuratively: anything that presents a false front

quiz.question: A cavalcade of limousines swept through the city centre for the president's arrival. What is a cavalcade?
options: A military escort of armed vehicles
A procession of vehicles or riders moving together
A ceremonial convoy assembled for a formal occasion
A large convoy of vehicles accompanied by a police escort
quiz.answer: A procession of vehicles or riders moving together
quiz.explanation:
A cavalcade is a formal procession — originally of horsemen, now of vehicles.
• cavalcade = a procession of riders, vehicles, or people
• From Italian: 'cavalcata' — a ride on horseback
• 'A cavalcade of stars' = a succession of notable people or events

quiz.question: The queen's robe was made from gold brocade. What is brocade?
options: A very fine, lightweight silk with a plain weave
A rich fabric with a raised pattern woven into it
A heavy woolen cloth used for formal ceremonial garments
An embroidered cloth decorated after the weaving process
quiz.answer: A rich fabric with a raised pattern woven into it
quiz.explanation:
Brocade is a rich, ornate fabric with a raised decorative pattern woven into it.
• brocade = a heavy fabric with an embossed or woven decorative pattern
• From Italian: 'broccato' — embossed cloth
• Often used in formal wear, furnishings, and ceremonial garments

quiz.question: Information cascaded down through the organization too slowly to be useful. What does 'cascade' mean here?
options: To flow or fall in a series of stages from top to bottom
To be distributed to all levels simultaneously
To be filtered and simplified as it passed downward
To accumulate at lower levels faster than it was processed
quiz.answer: To flow or fall in a series of stages from top to bottom
quiz.explanation:
To cascade means to fall or flow downward in stages — like water over a series of rocks.
• cascade = to fall in a series of stages; to pass along a chain
• From Italian: 'cascata' — a waterfall
• 'Cascade effect', 'cascading failure' — effects rippling down through a system

quiz.question: The renegade general had broken with the government and was leading his own forces. What is a renegade?
options: A general who had been dismissed from his post
A person who has deserted a cause and gone against it
An officer acting without orders from the chain of command
A commander whose extreme tactics were condemned by allies
quiz.answer: A person who has deserted a cause and gone against it
quiz.explanation:
A renegade is a person who abandons their principles, allegiance, or group — a traitor or rebel.
• renegade = someone who deserts a cause and acts against it
• From Spanish: 'renegado' — one who has denied (their faith)
• 'A renegade faction', 'going renegade' — leaving the fold and opposing it

quiz.question: The navy imposed a blockade on the enemy's ports. What is a blockade?
options: A surprise attack on vessels entering a harbor
A sealing off of a place to prevent entry or exit
A patrol of a coastline to intercept smugglers
A minefield laid across the approaches to a port, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A sealing off of a place to prevent entry or exit
quiz.explanation:
A blockade is a sealing off of a place — to prevent goods, people, or ships from entering or leaving.
• blockade = the blocking of access to a place
• From 'block' — to obstruct
• 'Run a blockade' = to break through; 'naval blockade' = ships sealing off a coast

quiz.question: The settlers built a stockade around their camp for protection. What is a stockade?
options: A stone wall reinforced with earth and timber
A defensive enclosure made of upright wooden stakes
A raised platform used to observe the surrounding area
A trench dug around a settlement to deter attackers
quiz.answer: A defensive enclosure made of upright wooden stakes
quiz.explanation:
A stockade is a defensive barrier made from upright wooden stakes or posts.
• stockade = an enclosure of upright wooden posts; a defensive palisade
• From Dutch: 'stakade' — a row of stakes
• Also: a stockade = a military prison enclosure

quiz.question: They picnicked in a sun-dappled glade in the middle of the forest. What is a glade?
options: An area of dense undergrowth beneath tall trees
An open, clear space within a wood or forest
A narrow path through dense forest vegetation
A clearing at the edge of a forest beside a stream
quiz.answer: An open, clear space within a wood or forest
quiz.explanation:
A glade is an open space in a forest — a clearing free of trees.
• glade = an open space within a wood, admitting light
• From Old English: 'glæd' — shining, bright
• A glade is enclosed by forest on all sides — it is within the wood, not at its edge

quiz.question: The press conference ended when reporters unleashed a fusillade of hostile questions. What is a fusillade?
options: A barrage of simultaneous, rapid-fire attacks
A coordinated protest from an organized group
A series of prepared questions delivered in turns
A hostile reaction from an audience to an answer
quiz.answer: A barrage of simultaneous, rapid-fire attacks
quiz.explanation:
A fusillade is a rapid, continuous outpouring — originally of gunfire, now of anything intense.
• fusillade = a rapid succession of shots or verbal attacks
• From French: 'fusil' — a flintlock musket
• 'A fusillade of criticism', 'a fusillade of bullets' — a rapid, overwhelming barrage

quiz.question: The film received the highest accolade at the festival — the Palme d'Or. What is an accolade?
options: A major prize or award given in recognition of achievement
Critical praise from reviewers and audiences
A nomination that acknowledges exceptional work
An honor presented at a ceremony to mark a career, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A major prize or award given in recognition of achievement
quiz.explanation:
An accolade is a prestigious award or honor — recognition of excellence.
• accolade = a mark of acknowledgment; a prestigious honor or award
• From French: 'accolade' — the embrace given when conferring knighthood
• 'The highest accolade', 'an accolade from peers' — recognition at the highest level

quiz.question: After dinner, the guests took a promenade along the waterfront. What is a promenade?
options: A leisurely walk, especially in a public place
A formal parade along a ceremonially decorated route
A boat trip along a river or coastal stretch
A relaxed gathering of people in an outdoor area
quiz.answer: A leisurely walk, especially in a public place
quiz.explanation:
A promenade is a leisurely walk for pleasure — especially in a public place.
• promenade = a walk for pleasure; also a paved public walkway
• From French: 'promener' — to take for a walk
• 'The prom' (short for promenade) = a seafront walkway; 'Promenade concerts' = the Proms

quiz.question: The colonnade along the front of the temple created a shaded walkway. What is a colonnade?
options: A decorative archway spanning the entrance to a building
A row of equally spaced columns supporting a roof
A series of carved stone panels along a building's base
A portico attached to the front of a classical building
quiz.answer: A row of equally spaced columns supporting a roof
quiz.explanation:
A colonnade is a row of regularly spaced columns supporting a roof or lintel.
• colonnade = a series of evenly spaced columns
• From Latin: 'columna' — a column
• Classic feature of Greek and Roman architecture; also modern civic buildings

quiz.question: The negotiations were a charade — both sides had already decided to walk away. What is a charade?
options: A meeting organized purely to satisfy a legal requirement
An absurd or pointless pretense — a hollow performance
A process designed to delay a decision indefinitely
A negotiation in which neither side acts in good faith
quiz.answer: An absurd or pointless pretense — a hollow performance
quiz.explanation:
A charade is a pointless pretense — going through the motions of something without any real substance.
• charade = an absurd pretense intended to create a false impression
• From French: 'charade' — a riddle
• 'Charades' (the game) involves acting out a word; a 'charade' = the whole thing is an act

quiz.question: The rough rope abraded the skin on his hands after hours of pulling. What does 'abrade' mean?
options: To cut deeply into the skin causing a wound
To wear or scrape away the surface by friction
To cause persistent irritation over a long period
To leave marks on the skin without breaking it
quiz.answer: To wear or scrape away the surface by friction
quiz.explanation:
To abrade means to scrape or wear away a surface through friction.
• abrade = to scrape or wear away by rubbing
• From Latin: 'abradere' — to scrape off
• 'Abrasive' = causing abrasion; 'abrasion' = the resulting wound or worn area

quiz.question: He masqueraded as a doctor for six years before being caught. What does 'masquerade' mean?
options: To pretend to be someone or something one is not
To practice medicine without the required qualifications
To use a false name and identity in official documents
To deceive others about one's level of expertise
quiz.answer: To pretend to be someone or something one is not
quiz.explanation:
To masquerade means to pretend to be something one is not — to wear a false identity.
• masquerade = to appear under a false identity; to pass off as something else
• From French: 'mascarade' — a masked ball
• 'Masquerade as an expert', 'masquerade as a friend' — wearing a false mask

quiz.question: Water boils at 100 degrees centigrade. What does 'centigrade' mean?
options: A scale that divides the range between freezing and boiling water into 100 equal parts
A metric unit measuring the intensity of heat
A scale referenced to the temperature of the human body, as was evident from all available evidence
A unit used only in scientific and laboratory contexts
quiz.answer: A scale that divides the range between freezing and boiling water into 100 equal parts
quiz.explanation:
Centigrade is a temperature scale with 100 degrees between the freezing and boiling points of water.
• centigrade = divided into 100 degrees (now more commonly called Celsius)
• From Latin: 'centum' (100) + 'gradus' (step)
• 0°C = freezing point of water; 100°C = boiling point at sea level

quiz.question: The manager's tirade lasted ten minutes and left the whole team shaken. What is a tirade?
options: A long, angry speech full of criticism and accusations
A disciplinary meeting held in front of other employees
A series of complaints delivered in a calm but firm manner
A heated argument that quickly escalated out of control
quiz.answer: A long, angry speech full of criticism and accusations
quiz.explanation:
A tirade is a long, vehement speech of criticism or denunciation.
• tirade = a prolonged angry outburst; a torrent of abuse or accusation
• From French: 'tirade' — a long speech
• More than a rant — a tirade is extended, sustained, and often directed at a specific target

quiz.question: She launched a crusade against food waste in schools. What does 'crusade' mean here?
options: A formal campaign involving public demonstrations
A vigorous and determined campaign for a cause
A petition signed by parents and teachers alike
A series of lectures and educational events on the topic
quiz.answer: A vigorous and determined campaign for a cause
quiz.explanation:
A crusade is a vigorous campaign — a sustained, determined effort for a cause.
• crusade = a passionate campaign; a concerted effort to achieve a goal
• From Latin: 'crux' — cross (from the medieval Crusades)
• 'Crusade against corruption', 'crusade for justice' — always energetic and committed

quiz.question: She waded through hundreds of emails to find the relevant one. What does 'wade' mean here?
options: To search systematically using a filtering system
To move through something with difficulty and effort
To read through quickly without stopping on each item
To ask a colleague to help sort through the backlog
quiz.answer: To move through something with difficulty and effort
quiz.explanation:
To wade means to move through something slowly and with effort — as if through deep water.
• wade = to walk through water or something difficult; to proceed laboriously
• From Old English: 'wadan' — to go, travel
• 'Wade through paperwork', 'wade into a fight' — moving despite resistance

quiz.question: France ceded Louisiana to the United States in 1803. What does 'cede' mean?
options: To formally transfer territory or rights to another party
To abandon territory following a military defeat, as the evidence showed
To lease territory under the terms of a treaty
To recognize the sovereignty of another nation
quiz.answer: To formally transfer territory or rights to another party
quiz.explanation:
To cede means to formally give up or transfer — especially territory.
• cede = to give up, surrender, or formally transfer to another
• From Latin: 'cedere' — to go, yield
• 'Cede territory', 'cede control' — a voluntary formal transfer

quiz.question: The prime minister acceded to pressure and agreed to hold an early election. What does 'accede' mean?
options: To formally agree or yield to a request or demand
To announce a change of policy under public scrutiny
To give in to pressure after a period of public resistance
To confirm a decision that had already been made privately
quiz.answer: To formally agree or yield to a request or demand
quiz.explanation:
To accede means to agree to a request — to yield and comply.
• accede = to agree; to formally assent to a request or condition
• From Latin: 'accedere' — to approach, agree
• 'Accede to the throne' = to become monarch; 'accede to demands' = to give in

quiz.question: The southern states seceded from the Union in 1860 and 1861. What does 'secede' mean?
options: To formally withdraw from a federation or political union
To declare independence from a colonial power
To refuse to recognize the authority of a central government
To expel a member state from an established union
quiz.answer: To formally withdraw from a federation or political union
quiz.explanation:
To secede means to formally withdraw from a political union.
• secede = to withdraw formally from an alliance, federation, or organization
• From Latin: 'secedere' — to go apart
• 'Secessionist movement' = one seeking withdrawal; secession = the act

quiz.question: She interceded on behalf of the dismissed worker and got his job back. What does 'intercede' mean?
options: To negotiate directly between two equally powerful parties
To intervene on someone's behalf and plead for them
To take legal action to reverse an unjust decision
To mediate between parties with competing interests
quiz.answer: To intervene on someone's behalf and plead for them
quiz.explanation:
To intercede means to intervene on behalf of another — to plead for someone.
• intercede = to act as an intermediary; to plead on behalf of another
• From Latin: 'intercedere' — to come between
• 'Intercede with the authorities', 'intercede for a prisoner' — speaking up for someone else

quiz.question: The new safety guidelines superseded all previous versions. What does 'supersede' mean?
options: To add additional requirements to existing guidelines
To replace something and make it no longer valid
To take precedence over conflicting guidelines
To update an existing document with new information
quiz.answer: To replace something and make it no longer valid
quiz.explanation:
To supersede means to take the place of something — making the previous version obsolete.
• supersede = to replace; to cause something to be abandoned
• From Latin: 'supersedere' — to sit above
• 'Superseded technology' = replaced and no longer used; the new thing takes over entirely

quiz.question: All competitors must abide by the rules. What does 'abide by' mean?
options: To accept the outcome of the rules' application
To follow and comply with something
To acknowledge the existence of a set of rules
To agree to the rules before participating
quiz.answer: To follow and comply with something
quiz.explanation:
To abide by means to obey or comply — to act in accordance with.
• abide by = to act in compliance with; to accept and follow
• From Old English: 'abidan' — to wait; to remain
• Also: 'can't abide' = cannot tolerate; 'abiding interest' = lasting, enduring

quiz.question: The farmer applied herbicide to kill the weeds without harming the crop. What is a herbicide?
options: A fertilizer that promotes the growth of specific plants
A chemical that kills plants — especially unwanted ones
A pesticide targeting insects that feed on crops
A soil treatment that prevents seed germination
quiz.answer: A chemical that kills plants — especially unwanted ones
quiz.explanation:
A herbicide is a chemical used to destroy plants, particularly weeds.
• herbicide = a substance used to kill or inhibit the growth of plants
• From Latin: 'herba' (plant) + 'caedere' (to kill)
• Related: insecticide (kills insects), fungicide (kills fungi), pesticide (kills pests generally)

quiz.question: The winemaker treated the vines with fungicide to prevent mildew. What does a fungicide do?
options: It prevents insects from damaging the root systems of vines
It kills or inhibits the growth of fungi
It protects the leaves from drying out in hot weather
It improves the soil conditions around the vines
quiz.answer: It kills or inhibits the growth of fungi
quiz.explanation:
A fungicide is a chemical that destroys or prevents the growth of fungi.
• fungicide = a substance that kills fungi or prevents their spread
• From Latin: 'fungus' + 'caedere' (to kill)
• Mildew, mold, and rust are fungal diseases controlled by fungicides

quiz.question: The hospital used a powerful germicide to sterilize the operating theatre. What is a germicide?
options: A substance that prevents bacteria from reproducing
A substance that destroys harmful microorganisms
A chemical that removes organic matter from surfaces
An antibiotic applied topically to prevent infection
quiz.answer: A substance that destroys harmful microorganisms
quiz.explanation:
A germicide is a substance that kills germs — bacteria and other harmful microorganisms.
• germicide = an agent capable of killing pathogenic microorganisms
• From Latin: 'germen' (bud, germ) + 'caedere' (to kill)
• Broader than antiseptic (used on skin) — germicides include disinfectants for surfaces

quiz.question: The company was fined for using an insecticide that harmed bee populations. What is an insecticide?
options: A chemical used to repel insects from crops
A chemical designed to kill insects
A trap baited to attract and capture pest insects
A biological agent that disrupts insect reproduction
quiz.answer: A chemical designed to kill insects
quiz.explanation:
An insecticide is a chemical used to kill insects.
• insecticide = a substance for killing insects
• From Latin: 'insectum' (insect) + 'caedere' (to kill)
• Neonicotinoids are a controversial class of insecticides linked to bee decline

quiz.question: Organic farmers avoid synthetic pesticides. What is a pesticide?
options: A chemical substance used to destroy or control any pest
A spray applied to crops to prevent fungal disease
A chemical that targets insects and rodents specifically, as the evidence showed
Any toxic substance used in agricultural production
quiz.answer: A chemical substance used to destroy or control any pest
quiz.explanation:
A pesticide is any substance used to kill or control pests — the broadest category.
• pesticide = a substance for destroying organisms harmful to crops or livestock
• From Latin: 'pestis' (plague) + 'caedere' (to kill)
• Includes insecticides, herbicides, fungicides, and rodenticides

quiz.question: The swan glided across the surface of the lake. What does 'glide' mean?
options: To move slowly and with great effort
To move smoothly and effortlessly without interruption
To skim lightly across a surface touching it briefly, as the evidence showed
To drift without any control over direction
quiz.answer: To move smoothly and effortlessly without interruption
quiz.explanation:
To glide means to move smoothly and continuously — effortless, flowing motion.
• glide = to move smoothly without perceptible effort
• From Old English: 'glidan' — to slip, glide
• 'Glider' = an aircraft without an engine; 'glide path' = smooth approach to landing

quiz.question: He had quit smoking for a year before backsliding during a period of stress. What does 'backslide' mean?
options: To return to a bad habit or lower standard after improvement
To fail to maintain progress made in a difficult situation, as the evidence showed
To give up entirely on a goal after one failure
To undo progress by making a series of poor decisions
quiz.answer: To return to a bad habit or lower standard after improvement
quiz.explanation:
To backslide means to relapse — to return to bad behavior after a period of improvement.
• backslide = to slip back into former bad habits or behavior
• From 'back' + 'slide' — sliding backward from the position gained
• 'Backsliding' in religious contexts = returning to sinful behavior after conversion

quiz.question: The politician's speech was full of bromides about unity and hope. What are bromides in this context?
options: Familiar, reassuring statements with no new meaning
Promises made without any intention of fulfilling them
Slogans designed to appeal to the widest possible audience
Vague statements that could be interpreted in multiple ways
quiz.answer: Familiar, reassuring statements with no new meaning
quiz.explanation:
A bromide is a dull, unoriginal remark — a cliché used to calm or reassure.
• bromide = a trite, boring saying; a platitude offered to calm or placate
• From the sedative chemical bromide — used to calm patients
• 'A bromide response' = a predictable reassurance that says nothing new

quiz.question: Critics derided the film as the worst of the decade. What does 'deride' mean?
options: To dismiss something with contemptuous mockery
To criticize something through a formal review
To compare something unfavorably to similar works
To express strong disappointment at an expected standard
quiz.answer: To dismiss something with contemptuous mockery
quiz.explanation:
To deride means to dismiss with contempt — to mock and ridicule scornfully.
• deride = to laugh at contemptuously; to treat as unworthy of respect
• From Latin: 'deridere' — to laugh at
• 'Derisive laughter', 'derision' = contemptuous mockery

quiz.question: She has resided in the same house for over forty years. What does 'reside' mean?
options: To pay taxes in a country over a long period
To live somewhere permanently or for a long time
To own property in a particular location
To maintain legal address in a specific jurisdiction
quiz.answer: To live somewhere permanently or for a long time
quiz.explanation:
To reside means to live somewhere as one's permanent or long-term home.
• reside = to have one's home in a particular place
• From Latin: 'residere' — to sit back, settle
• 'Resident', 'residence', 'residential' — all relate to where one lives

quiz.question: She presided over the committee meeting as chairperson. What does 'preside' mean?
options: To attend a meeting as the most senior person present
To lead and be in charge of a meeting or ceremony
To cast the deciding vote when a meeting is deadlocked
To prepare and circulate the agenda before a meeting
quiz.answer: To lead and be in charge of a meeting or ceremony
quiz.explanation:
To preside means to be in charge — to direct a meeting or ceremony with authority.
• preside = to be in the position of authority at a meeting or event
• From Latin: 'praesidere' — to sit before, guard
• 'Presiding judge', 'preside over talks' — leading from the chair

quiz.question: Keats wrote an ode to a Grecian urn. What is an ode?
options: A narrative poem telling the story of a historical event
A lyric poem of praise or meditation on a subject
A poem written to be sung to music at a formal occasion
A poem composed in couplets following a strict rhyme scheme
quiz.answer: A lyric poem of praise or meditation on a subject
quiz.explanation:
An ode is a lyric poem — typically exalted in style, addressed to a person or thing.
• ode = a type of lyric poem meant to be sung; often elaborate and dignified
• From Greek: 'oide' — a song
• Famous odes: Keats, Shelley, Horace; 'Ode to Joy' = Beethoven's / Schiller's poem

quiz.question: The dark clouds on the horizon boded ill for the outdoor concert. What does 'bode' mean?
options: To confirm that an event would definitely take place, as the evidence showed
To be an omen — to portend good or ill for the future
To make the cancellation of the event more likely
To cast a shadow of doubt over the plans being made
quiz.answer: To be an omen — to portend good or ill for the future
quiz.explanation:
To bode means to be an omen — to indicate that something good or bad is coming.
• bode = to be a sign or portent of a future event
• From Old English: 'bodian' — to announce, foretell
• 'Bodes well' = a good sign; 'bodes ill' = a bad sign

quiz.question: A sense of dread seemed to forebode disaster even before anything had gone wrong. What does 'forebode' mean?
options: To cause a premonition or sense of impending trouble
To predict a specific event with some certainty
To create anxiety through the absence of information, as the evidence showed
To warn formally that a risk has been identified
quiz.answer: To cause a premonition or sense of impending trouble
quiz.explanation:
To forebode means to foreshadow or give a premonition of — usually something bad.
• forebode = to be a warning sign of future evil; to presage
• From 'fore-' (before) + 'bode' (to be an omen)
• 'Foreboding' = a feeling that something bad is about to happen

quiz.question: The miners struck a rich lode of silver ore deep underground. What is a lode?
options: A large underground pocket of pressurized gas
A vein of metal ore running through rock
A horizontal layer of mineral-bearing rock
An underground stream carrying dissolved minerals
quiz.answer: A vein of metal ore running through rock
quiz.explanation:
A lode is a vein of metal ore running through rock — a seam of mineral deposit.
• lode = a vein of metallic ore in rock
• From Old English: 'lad' — a way, course
• 'Mother lode' = the main or richest source; 'lodestone' = a naturally magnetic rock

quiz.question: The metal pipes corroded over decades, eventually springing leaks. What does 'corrode' mean?
options: To wear away gradually through chemical action
To crack and fracture under sustained pressure
To expand and deform when subjected to extreme cold
To weaken progressively due to mechanical stress
quiz.answer: To wear away gradually through chemical action
quiz.explanation:
To corrode means to eat away gradually — through chemical reaction such as rust or acid.
• corrode = to destroy or damage slowly by chemical action
• From Latin: 'corrodere' — to gnaw away
• 'Corrosion' = the result; 'corrosive' = causing corrosion; also figurative: corrosive cynicism

quiz.question: The gallery specialized in avant-garde art that many visitors found baffling. What does 'avant-garde' describe?
options: Art that is expensive and targeted at wealthy collectors
Work that pushes beyond conventional boundaries into new territory
Art that requires specialist knowledge to appreciate
Work that challenges social and political norms deliberately, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Work that pushes beyond conventional boundaries into new territory
quiz.explanation:
Avant-garde describes work that is innovative and ahead of the mainstream — on the cutting edge.
• avant-garde = pioneering or experimental; ahead of its time
• From French: 'avant-garde' — vanguard, the advance troops
• 'Avant-garde music', 'avant-garde fashion' — breaking ahead of accepted convention

quiz.question: A blood clot had occluded the artery, blocking blood flow. What does 'occlude' mean?
options: To damage the inner lining of a vessel
To block or close off a passage completely
To narrow a passage without fully sealing it
To trigger a spasm that temporarily restricts flow
quiz.answer: To block or close off a passage completely
quiz.explanation:
To occlude means to block — to close off a passage completely.
• occlude = to close, block, or obstruct
• From Latin: 'occludere' — to shut up
• 'Occluded artery', 'occluded front' (weather) — blocked or closed off

quiz.question: Her injury precluded her from competing in the championship. What does 'preclude' mean?
options: To make her participation significantly more difficult
To prevent something from happening by prior action
To require her to withdraw after an official assessment
To disqualify her once the competition had begun
quiz.answer: To prevent something from happening by prior action
quiz.explanation:
To preclude means to prevent something in advance — to make it impossible.
• preclude = to prevent or make impossible in advance
• From Latin: 'praecludere' — to close before
• 'Preclude the possibility', 'preclude further discussion' — closing off before it starts

quiz.question: Women were excluded from membership of the club for over a century. What does 'exclude' mean?
options: To deny entry or membership to a person or group
To restrict full participation without banning membership
To make membership unpleasant through discriminatory rules
To fail to invite a group through oversight rather than intent
quiz.answer: To deny entry or membership to a person or group
quiz.explanation:
To exclude means to keep out — to prevent from entering or participating.
• exclude = to shut out; to prevent from inclusion
• From Latin: 'excludere' — to shut out
• 'Exclusion zone', 'social exclusion' — kept outside, denied access

quiz.question: The tense border skirmishes were a prelude to full-scale war. What is a prelude?
options: An event that causes or triggers a larger event
An introductory event that leads into something larger
A period of tension before a conflict breaks out
A warning sign that is ignored until it is too late, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: An introductory event that leads into something larger
quiz.explanation:
A prelude is an introductory event — something that precedes and leads into something bigger.
• prelude = an action or event that introduces something more important
• From Latin: 'praeludere' — to play beforehand
• Musical prelude = an introductory piece; figurative prelude = a forerunner

quiz.question: The speaker alluded to recent scandals without naming anyone directly. What does 'allude' mean?
options: To accuse someone indirectly in a public setting
To refer to something indirectly without naming it
To hint that more serious allegations might follow, as the evidence showed
To mention something in passing as a minor point
quiz.answer: To refer to something indirectly without naming it
quiz.explanation:
To allude means to refer to something indirectly — without naming or specifying it directly.
• allude = to make an indirect reference
• From Latin: 'alludere' — to play with
• 'Allusion' = the indirect reference; 'allusive' = full of indirect references

quiz.question: There was a brief musical interlude between the second and third acts. What is an interlude?
options: A short intermission that marks the end of one section and the start of another
A pause interval inserted between sections of a performance
A piece of music performed to fill a gap in a program
A transition scene connecting two parts of a dramatic work
quiz.answer: A pause interval inserted between sections of a performance
quiz.explanation:
An interlude is a pause or brief performance between the main sections of something.
• interlude = an intervening period or event; a pause between sections
• From Latin: 'inter-' (between) + 'ludere' (to play)
• 'Musical interlude', 'a brief interlude of peace' — something happening in the in-between

quiz.question: The hillside had been denuded of trees by decades of logging. What does 'denude' mean?
options: To strip bare — to remove all covering from a surface
To damage irreversibly through sustained exploitation
To change the character of a landscape through removal
To remove vegetation making the soil vulnerable to erosion
quiz.answer: To strip bare — to remove all covering from a surface
quiz.explanation:
To denude means to strip bare — to remove all natural covering.
• denude = to strip of covering; to make bare
• From Latin: 'denudare' — to lay bare
• 'Denuded hillside', 'denuded of power' — stripped of what should be there

quiz.question: She was called a prude for refusing to laugh at the crude jokes. What is a prude?
options: A person who holds others to unrealistically high standards
A person who is excessively proper and easily shocked by impropriety
A person who refuses to participate in social activities generally, as the evidence showed
A person who expresses moral disapproval of others publicly
quiz.answer: A person who is excessively proper and easily shocked by impropriety
quiz.explanation:
A prude is a person who is excessively proper — easily offended by anything indelicate.
• prude = a person who is easily shocked by sexual matters or indelicate language
• From French: 'prudefemme' — a respectable woman (later used mockingly)
• 'Prudish' = excessively proper; prudery = the behavior of a prude

quiz.question: His opinions obtrude into every conversation whether they are wanted or not. What does 'obtrude' mean?
options: To force oneself or one's views on others uninvited
To introduce a topic that diverts a conversation
To express an opinion loudly and with great confidence
To raise issues that others would prefer to avoid
quiz.answer: To force oneself or one's views on others uninvited
quiz.explanation:
To obtrude means to push forward unwantedly — to intrude on others' attention.
• obtrude = to impose on others without being invited; to thrust forward
• From Latin: 'obtrudere' — to thrust against
• 'Obtrusive' = attracting attention in an unwelcome way

quiz.question: The machine extruded aluminium into long thin bars. What does 'extrude' mean?
options: To force a material through a shaped opening to form a specific profile
To compress a material into a mold at high temperature
To cut a material into sections using a rotary blade
To flatten a material by passing it between heavy rollers, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To force a material through a shaped opening to form a specific profile
quiz.explanation:
To extrude means to push or force material through a die or shaped opening.
• extrude = to shape a material by forcing it through an opening
• From Latin: 'extrudere' — to push out
• 'Extruded plastic', 'extruded pasta' — shaped by forcing through a mold

quiz.question: The law had fallen into desuetude and was no longer enforced by anyone. What is desuetude?
options: A formal process of repealing outdated legislation
The state of no longer being used or practiced
The period during which a law awaits formal repeal
A legal doctrine allowing old laws to be disregarded
quiz.answer: The state of no longer being used or practiced
quiz.explanation:
Desuetude is the state of no longer being in use — having fallen into disuse.
• desuetude = a state of disuse; the condition of being no longer practiced
• From Latin: 'desuetudo' — disuse
• 'Fall into desuetude' = to stop being used without being formally abolished

quiz.question: He showed great solicitude for the welfare of his elderly neighbor. What is solicitude?
options: Anxious concern and attentive care for another's well-being
Practical help given to someone who cannot manage alone
A sense of duty toward those who are weaker or vulnerable
Regular visits made to ensure someone is coping adequately, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Anxious concern and attentive care for another's well-being
quiz.explanation:
Solicitude is anxious or careful concern for someone's well-being.
• solicitude = careful concern; anxious care for another
• From Latin: 'solicitudo' — anxiety, concern
• More intense than kindness — solicitude implies active worry and attention

quiz.question: She retreated to a remote cottage to enjoy a week of solitude. What is solitude?
options: The peace that comes from being in a natural environment
The state of being alone — especially by choice
Freedom from the demands and noise of everyday life
An extended period of rest away from responsibilities
quiz.answer: The state of being alone — especially by choice
quiz.explanation:
Solitude is the state of being alone — often chosen for peace or reflection.
• solitude = being alone; seclusion from the company of others
• From Latin: 'solitudo' — loneliness
• Positive connotation unlike 'loneliness' — solitude is often voluntary and valued

quiz.question: The physicist measured the amplitude of the wave. What is amplitude?
options: The frequency at which the wave repeats itself
The maximum extent of a vibration from its resting position
The speed at which a wave travels through a medium
The wavelength measured between two consecutive peaks, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The maximum extent of a vibration from its resting position
quiz.explanation:
Amplitude is the maximum displacement of a wave — how far it moves from its resting point.
• amplitude = the maximum extent of a vibration; the height of a wave
• From Latin: 'amplitude' — breadth, extent
• In radio: amplitude modulation (AM) encodes sound by varying wave height

quiz.question: The market offered a plenitude of fresh produce from across the region. What is plenitude?
options: A wide variety of products from many different suppliers
An abundance — a full and generous supply
A concentration of the finest produce in one location
An unusually large seasonal harvest made available locally
quiz.answer: An abundance — a full and generous supply
quiz.explanation:
Plenitude is fullness and abundance — having more than enough.
• plenitude = an abundance; a full and copious supply
• From Latin: 'plenitudo' — fullness
• Similar to 'plenty' but more formal and suggesting complete fullness

quiz.question: Scientists did not fully grasp the magnitude of the problem until the data arrived. What is magnitude?
options: The underlying causes that made the problem hard to solve
The great size or importance of something
The speed at which a problem was developing
The complexity that made the problem difficult to analyze
quiz.answer: The great size or importance of something
quiz.explanation:
Magnitude is the great size or scale of something — its extent or importance.
• magnitude = great size or extent; importance
• From Latin: 'magnitudo' — greatness
• 'Of the first magnitude' = of the greatest importance; earthquake magnitude = size on Richter scale

quiz.question: He was disbarred for moral turpitude — behavior utterly unbecoming of an officer of the court. What is turpitude?
options: Depraved or wicked behavior that violates moral standards
A pattern of dishonesty extending over many years
Conduct that brings a profession into serious disrepute, as the evidence showed
A deliberate breach of a professional code of ethics
quiz.answer: Depraved or wicked behavior that violates moral standards
quiz.explanation:
Turpitude is wickedness or depravity — conduct that violates moral standards.
• turpitude = wickedness; shameful or depraved behavior
• From Latin: 'turpitudo' — baseness, from 'turpis' — base, foul
• 'Moral turpitude' = the legal term for conduct considered contrary to community standards
